label: ai
topic: Students should openly express their political views and engage in debate on campus
text:
The morning after the election, the campus was quieter than I expected. Not silent, exactly, but muted, as though everyone had agreed overnight to speak in lower registers. In the dining hall, people ate with their heads down. A few tables had the particular strained energy of groups trying very hard to have a normal conversation. Nobody was discussing what had happened, at least not loudly.

I found this more alarming than any protest would have been.

I come from a place where political disagreement is not a theoretical concept explored in seminar readings but a daily, material reality. My hometown votes Republican by margins that make the state's overall lean look moderate. My family does not. Growing up, I learned early that expressing a political opinion in the wrong setting carried consequences that ranged from awkward silence to genuine social exclusion. I learned to calibrate, to read rooms, to keep certain views private unless I was confident of my audience.

I came to this university partly because I expected a different environment. The campus leans progressive, the faculty leans progressive, the institutional communications lean progressive. I assumed this would mean more openness, more debate, more willingness to engage with disagreement as a productive exercise rather than a threat.

What I found was a different version of the same problem. The political valence had changed, but the underlying dynamic was identical: a dominant consensus that discourages dissent, not through formal prohibition but through the social costs attached to stepping outside it. Conservative students here describe the same experience I had at home, the same room-reading, the same self-censorship, the same careful calibration of what to say and to whom.

The result is that political conversation on campus happens almost exclusively within ideological silos. Progressive students talk to progressive students about progressive priorities and reinforce each other's assumptions. Conservative students, fewer in number and more cautious about visibility, do the same within their own circles. The two groups interact in classes and dining halls and dormitories, but they do not, for the most part, discuss politics with each other. They discuss the weather, or assignments, or anything else that does not risk the fragile social equilibrium.

This is not healthy, and it is not what a university education is supposed to produce. The value of spending four years in close proximity to people from different backgrounds and with different perspectives is precisely that it forces you to articulate and defend your views against challenges you would not encounter in a more homogeneous environment. If we never engage in that process, we graduate with opinions that have never been tested and convictions that have never been examined, which is a poor foundation for citizenship in a democracy that requires its members to negotiate genuine disagreement.

I am not arguing that all opinions deserve equal respect or that every political position is equally defensible. I am arguing that the process of engaging with positions you find wrong is more valuable than the comfort of avoiding them. I am arguing that a campus culture in which a significant portion of students feel unable to express their political views openly is a campus culture that has failed at one of its core educational missions.

The morning after the next election, I would like to walk into the dining hall and hear people arguing. Not shouting, not insulting, but arguing, with the kind of earnest, imperfect, sometimes uncomfortable engagement that democracy requires. That would mean the university had done its job.
